cards.question: What is Inversion of Control (IoC) in Spring?
cards.answer: IoC means Spring manages object creation and wiring, so classes receive dependencies instead of creating them manually.

cards.question: When should you use constructor injection over field injection?
cards.answer: Constructor injection is preferred because dependencies are explicit, immutable, and test-friendly; field injection hides required dependencies.

cards.question: What is the difference between singleton and prototype bean scopes?
cards.answer: Singleton creates one shared bean per container, while prototype creates a new bean instance each time it is requested.

cards.question: How does @Primary help when multiple beans of the same type exist?
cards.answer: @Primary marks one bean as the default candidate during autowiring unless another bean is explicitly selected with @Qualifier.

cards.question: What is the purpose of @Configuration classes?
cards.answer: @Configuration classes define bean creation methods with @Bean and centralize wiring logic for the application context.

cards.question: Why is ApplicationContext preferred over BeanFactory in most applications?
cards.answer: ApplicationContext extends BeanFactory and adds enterprise features like events, i18n, AOP integration, and eager singleton initialization.
